L1.area: Image processing and pixel data manipulation layer

L2.description: An integer overflow vulnerability exists in image processing where attacker-controlled size parameters are used in multiplication operations for memory allocation without proper overflow checks. When processing images with negative or very large dimension values, the integer overflow results in undersized memory allocations, leading to a heap-based buffer overflow that could allow remote attackers to cause denial of service or potentially execute arbitrary code.

L3.description: An integer overflow vulnerability exists in the image resampling logic where attacker-controlled output dimension parameters are multiplied with filter coefficient counts for buffer allocation without proper overflow checks. Negative or very large new size values cause the multiplication to wrap, resulting in undersized heap allocations and subsequent buffer overflows during pixel data processing.

ground_truth.reason: An integer overflow in the horizontal image resampling function allows negative or very large size values to cause multiplication wrapping in buffer allocation calculations, resulting in undersized heap allocations and subsequent out-of-bounds writes when processing pixel data.
